title: Why the brain confuses arousal states and misreads safety as threat
author: Kalesh
excerpt:

metaDescription:
Your nervous system is a master of disguise. And sometimes it lies to you. 

 I was sitting in my living room the other day, completely still, reading a b...
htmlContent:
Your nervous system is a master of disguise. And sometimes it lies to you.

I was sitting in my living room the other day, completely still, reading a book. Nothing was happening. No loud noises. No conflict. No danger. And yet my heart was pounding like I'd just been chased. My hands were cold. My jaw was clenched. My brain was screaming run while my environment was whispering you're fine.

I've learned to recognize this now. But for years, I thought I was just broken. I thought my brain was defective. I thought I was the only person who could feel terrified while sitting safely on a couch.

Here's the thing. I'm not broken. And neither are you. What we're experiencing is a fundamental design feature of the human nervous system. A feature that kept our ancestors alive. A feature that, in a modern world full of triggers and trauma histories, can feel like it's working against us.

Let's talk about why your brain confuses arousal states. Why it misreads safety as threat. And what that means for your body, your relationships, and your life.

The Polyvagal Theory: Your nervous system's secret language

Stephen Porges, a neuroscientist who has spent decades studying the vagus nerve, gave us something . He called it the Polyvagal Theory. And it changed everything I thought I knew about fear.

Porges discovered that our nervous system doesn't just have one threat response. It has three distinct pathways. And they operate on a hierarchy. Think of it like a ladder.

At the top is social engagement. This is your ventral vagal state. It's when you feel safe, connected, present. Your face is relaxed. Your voice has range. You can make eye contact and actually hear what someone is saying. This is the state we're designed to live in most of the time.

But if something feels off, your nervous system drops to the next rung. This is sympathetic activation. Fight or flight. Your heart rate goes up. Your pupils dilate. Your blood moves to your big muscles. You're ready to move. To fight. To flee. To do something.

And if neither of those works, if you're trapped or overwhelmed, you drop to the bottom rung. Dorsal vagal shutdown. Immobilization. Freeze. Collapse. Your body goes numb. Your mind goes fuzzy. You dissociate. It's your system's way of playing dead until the danger passes.

Here's where it gets tricky. These states are not just responses to actual threat. They're responses to perceived threat. And your nervous system is constantly scanning your environment, your body, and your social context for cues of safety or danger. Porges calls this neuroception. It happens below conscious awareness. You don't choose it. It chooses you.

So when your neuroception picks up something that looks like a cue for danger, even if you consciously know you're safe, your body reacts. It drops down the ladder. It activates survival responses. And it does this in a fraction of a second, way before your thinking brain can catch up.

This is why you can be safe and still feel terrified. Your nervous system is running on old data. On past trauma. On patterns that were wired in years or decades ago.

Why arousal feels like threat: The body's mixed signals

Here's another layer. Your body uses the same physiological signals for very different experiences.

Heart racing. Breathing quickening. Sweating. Flushed skin. Muscle tension. These are the hallmarks of fear. But they're also the hallmarks of excitement. Of joy. Of sexual arousal. Of anger. Of exercise. Of a really good cup of coffee.

Your brain doesn't automatically know the difference. It has to interpret these signals based on context. And if your context includes a history of trauma, your brain's default interpretation is often danger.

Think about it. You're at a party. Someone you're attracted to walks up to you. Your heart starts pounding. Your face gets warm. Your breath catches. That could be excitement. That could be attraction. That could be the beginning of something beautiful.

But if your nervous system has learned that vulnerability means danger, that connection means betrayal, that intimacy means loss, then those same physical sensations get interpreted as threat. You feel scared. You want to run. You shut down or you lash out. And you miss the moment entirely.

Peter Levine, the founder of Somatic Experiencing, talks about this extensively. He describes how trauma gets trapped in the body. How incomplete survival responses leave a residue. How your system stays stuck in a state of high alert, waiting for a threat that already happened.

I remember the first time I realized this was happening to me. I was on a date. A good date. The person was kind. We were laughing. I felt something warm in my chest. And then, out of nowhere, I felt nauseous. My vision narrowed. I wanted to leave. I made an excuse and went home. I lay in bed wondering what was wrong with me.

Nothing was wrong with me. My nervous system was just doing its job. It was trying to protect me from the vulnerability of connection. It was confusing the arousal of intimacy with the arousal of danger. Because in my history, they had been the same thing.

This confusion doesn't just affect relationships. It affects everything. You can't exercise without feeling panicked. You can't raise your voice in excitement without triggering a shutdown. You can't enjoy a thrilling movie without feeling like you're going to die. The line between arousal and threat gets erased.

And the wellness industry? It doesn't help. So many programs tell you to "just breathe" or "stay present" or "feel your feelings." But when your nervous system is in survival mode, breathing can feel like drowning. Staying present can feel like torture. Feeling your feelings can feel like being ripped open.

You don't need more pressure to be calm. You need to understand why your system is reacting the way it is. You need to rebuild the wiring.

How trauma rewires the brain's threat detection system

Bessel van der Kolk has spent his career studying how trauma changes the brain. His research shows that trauma survivors have a hyperactive amygdala, the brain's smoke detector. It goes off at the slightest hint of smoke, even when there's no fire. And it stays on longer than it should.

At the same time, the medial prefrontal cortex, the part of the brain that can say "hey, we're actually safe right now," gets weaker. It has less ability to calm the amygdala down. So you're left with a smoke detector that's screaming and a fire department that can't do its job.

This is not a character flaw. This is not a lack of willpower. This is neurobiology. Your brain has been rewired by experience.

A book that many trauma survivors find genuinely helpful is The Body Keeps the Score (paid link). Van der Kolk lays out the science in a way that makes you feel seen, not pathologized. He explains why talk therapy alone often isn't enough, because the trauma is stored in the body, not just in the narrative.

So what does this mean for the confusion between arousal states? It means your nervous system has become a hyper-vigilant guard dog. It barks at everything. The mailman. The wind. A car backfiring. A friend's raised voice. A touch that comes too suddenly. A moment of silence that feels too heavy.

It also means your system has lost the ability to accurately read the room. You misread neutral faces as angry. You misread gentle touch as intrusive. You misread excitement as danger. You're living in a world that feels unsafe, even when it isn't.

This is exhausting. It's isolating. It makes you want to stay home, alone, where you can control every variable. But even then, your own body can feel like a threat. Your own heartbeat can feel like an alarm.

Something worth exploring for those drawn to body-based healing is In an Unspoken Voice (paid link) by Peter Levine. It dives into how the body holds trauma and how we can gently release it without being retraumatized.

Practical ways to start untangling the confusion

So what do you do when your nervous system is lying to you? When your body says danger but your mind says safe?

First, you stop trying to argue with your nervous system. You can't reason your way out of a survival response. Telling yourself "I'm safe, I'm safe, I'm safe" while your heart is pounding is like trying to put out a fire with a whisper. It doesn't work.

Instead, you start with the body. You orient to safety. You look around the room. You find something neutral or pleasant. A plant. A lamp. The shape of a window. You let your eyes rest there. You breathe, but you don't force it. You just let your breath find its own rhythm.

You also start to notice the difference between arousal and threat. This takes practice. It takes curiosity. You ask yourself: "Is this sensation familiar from a past experience of danger? Or is it something new? Could it be something else?"

One supplement that many people find calming for their nervous system is Doctors Best Magnesium Glycinate (paid link). Magnesium supports the nervous system and can help reduce the physical sensations of hyperarousal. It's not a cure, but it can take the edge off while you do the deeper work.

You also need to rebuild your capacity for regulation. This means finding small moments of safety throughout your day. A warm cup of tea. A few minutes of rocking. A hand on your own heart. These tiny acts tell your nervous system: "We're here. We're okay. We can slow down."

A compassionate reframing of trauma that resonates with many is What Happened to You? (paid link) by Oprah Winfrey and Bruce Perry. It shifts the question from "what's wrong with you" to "what happened to you." That shift alone can change how you relate to your own nervous system.

Stay with me here. This work is slow. It's not linear. Some days you'll feel like you're making progress. Other days you'll feel like you're back at square one. That's normal. That's the nature of healing a nervous system that has been wired for survival.

You're not trying to erase your history. You're not trying to become a person who never feels fear. You're trying to build new pathways. New interpretations. New possibilities for your body to experience arousal without immediately assuming danger.

The open ending: Living with a nervous system that sometimes lies

I still have moments where my brain confuses arousal with threat. I still sit on my couch with a racing heart, wondering if I'm okay. The difference is that now I know what's happening. I can name it. I can stay with it without adding a layer of shame on top.

"Oh, there's my nervous system doing its thing. It thinks this excitement is danger. It's confused. That's okay. I can let it be confused and still be here."

That's the work. Not getting rid of the confusion. But learning to live with it. Learning to hold it. Learning to say "yes, I feel this, and I'm still safe."

Your nervous system is not your enemy. It's a protector that learned its job in a dangerous world. It's doing its best with the information it has. And with time, patience, and the right tools, you can teach it a new language. A language where arousal can mean pleasure. Where connection can mean safety. Where your body can finally rest.

It's possible. I'm living proof. And so can you.
